- Changing saved sidebar width persistence
- Redesigning More Filters
acceptance_criteria:
- [ ] At narrow Species Catalog Workbench sidebar widths, Climate Zone reserves enough height for all wrapped ribbons, including Boreal.
- [ ] No wrapped choice ribbon paints behind Habit or any following always-visible filter row.
- [ ] Other always-visible choice filter rows keep natural wrapping behavior and reserve their wrapped height.
- [ ] The combined filter region remains the only capped/scrollable filter container; individual choice rows do not rely on visible overflow to expose wrapped content.
- [ ] Existing sidebar default width and saved width persistence behavior remain unchanged.
- [ ] More Filters chip wrapping behavior is not regressed.
- [ ] Focused frontend regression coverage protects the row-height reservation behavior, preferably through rendered geometry; if the local test environment cannot compute layout, the test must explicitly guard against the layout combination that allowed wrapped ribbons to escape their row.
- [ ] Required frontend checks are run, including focused Vitest coverage and `cd desktop/web && npx tsc --noEmit`.
close_reason: Implemented flex-based Species Catalog filter rows so wrapped choice ribbons reserve row height; verified with focused filter-strip Vitest coverage and npx tsc --noEmit.

title: Fix Species Catalog filter strip row wrapping
description:
## Slice Summary
Fix the always-visible Species Catalog Filter strip so choice rows grow and shrink naturally as the Species Catalog Workbench sidebar width changes.

## User Value
Users resizing the Species Catalog Workbench can see all visible Climate Zone and other choice ribbons without rows appearing fixed at one or two lines.

## Out of Scope
Do not change Species Catalog request contracts, filter semantics, filter option ordering, sidebar width persistence, or the More Filters panel behavior except as needed to preserve shared wrapping expectations.
design:
## Agent Brief
**Category:** bug
**Summary:** Make always-visible Species Catalog Filter choice rows resize naturally with the Species Catalog Workbench sidebar.

**Current behavior:**
The always-visible Species Catalog Filter strip appears to keep Climate Zone at a fixed two-row height across practical sidebar width changes. Other choice rows can also appear locked to a single row. The implementation relies on a parent strip layout that does not behave like the natural wrapping chip groups used in More Filters, so row-height changes are not reliably visible when the panel width changes.

**Desired behavior:**
Each always-visible Species Catalog Filter row owns its own height. Choice rows wrap like More Filters chip groups: narrower sidebars may produce more ribbon rows, wider sidebars may produce fewer rows, and all visible ribbons remain available. The combined filter region may remain capped and scrollable, but individual choice rows must not clip or force a fixed row count.

**Key interfaces:**
- Species Catalog Workbench filter strip projection
- Always-visible Species Catalog Filter rows
- Shared filter chip/ribbon presentation
- Species Catalog sidebar width and resize behavior
- Frontend layout regression tests for Species Catalog filters

**Out of scope:**
- Changing Species Catalog search request shape or backend filtering behavior
- Changing Climate Zone option values, labels, translations, or ordering
- Reworking the More Filters panel beyond preserving matching wrapping behavior
- Changing saved sidebar width persistence semantics
acceptance_criteria:
- [ ] Always-visible choice filter rows use a real per-row layout container that can grow independently with wrapped ribbons.
- [ ] Climate Zone shows all visible ribbons at narrow, default, and widened Species Catalog Workbench sidebar widths, with row height decreasing or increasing according to available width.
- [ ] Other always-visible choice rows keep the same natural wrapping behavior and do not clip to a fixed one-row height.
- [ ] The combined filter region remains capped or scrollable only at the region level; individual choice rows do not use fixed height, row-level max height, or overflow clipping to hide ribbons.
- [ ] Existing sidebar default width and saved width persistence behavior remain unchanged.
- [ ] Focused frontend regression coverage protects the filter-strip layout contract, including the absence of the old fixed-row pattern.
- [ ] Required frontend checks are run, including the focused Vitest coverage and `cd desktop/web && npx tsc --noEmit`.
close_reason: Fixed Species Catalog filter strip row wrapping by replacing the parent-grid/display-contents strip layout with real per-row containers that preserve natural flex-wrapped choice ribbons. Verified with npm test -- --run src/__tests__/filter-strip.test.tsx, npx tsc --noEmit, and git diff --check.

title: Remember responsive Species Catalog Workbench width
description: Reduce the default Species Catalog Workbench sidebar footprint and make its always-visible filters respond to the remembered panel width.
acceptance_criteria: First-use sidebar width defaults to clamp(320px, 35vw, 90vw) without a fixed pixel cap; explicit resize is remembered for the current and future app sessions through settings; always-visible Species Catalog Filter rows wrap to content based on actual panel width without clipping; design docs record the sidebar width and filter wrapping rules.
notes:
Reopened after design review: the filter area needs a combined natural-height region with an extreme-height cap; specs must distinguish filter rows, filter categories, and filter choices. Second diagnosis: real CSS-module probe shows choice rows reflow only when actual panel width changes. The first-use clamp(320px, 35vw, 520px) cap kept large viewports at 520px, where Climate Zone is exactly two rows and shorter rows are one row.
Reopened after user report: visible filter-row breakpoints still feel stuck because Climate Zone has seven choices and the strip grid uses an 80px minimum choice track, keeping it at two rows across common remembered/sidebar widths while shorter rows remain one row.
Reopened after user comparison with More Filters: More Filters chip rows use natural flex wrapping, while the always-visible strip still used equal-track grid layout, so the row height did not follow ribbon content like the working implementation.
close_reason: Aligned the always-visible Species Catalog Filter choice rows with the working More Filters ribbon behavior: removed equal-track grid layout and strip-only chip sizing, so rows naturally grow and shrink from flex-wrapped FilterChip content. Verified with npm test -- filter-strip, npx tsc --noEmit, git diff --check, and a temporary headless Firefox probe at 320/420/520/700px.

title: Deepen interaction seams for Scene Edit tools and Timeline Action
description: Refactor the existing Scene Edit tool module seam and Timeline Action workbench so callers learn less about lifecycle, event ordering, and implementation details. Keep behavior stable while moving complexity behind deeper module interfaces.
design: Favor long-term locality and leverage over minimum diff size. Preserve existing public canvas runtime surfaces, Planning Projection authority, Target Presentation separation, and document edit semantics.
acceptance_criteria: SceneInteractionController delegates concrete tool construction and lifecycle knowledge behind a smaller tool module seam; Timeline Action callers cross a smaller workbench interface instead of assembling refs and delegates; existing user-equivalent behavior is covered by focused tests and existing boundary tests are updated.
close_reason: Shipped deeper Scene Edit tool-module and Timeline Action interaction-workbench seams while preserving public runtime and planning surfaces. Verified with cd desktop/web && npx tsc --noEmit, focused Vitest boundary/runtime coverage, and cd desktop/web && npm test.

title: Move Annotation editing behind the Scene Edit tool seam
description:
## Slice Summary
Migrate Annotation Text placement and editor lifecycle behind the internal Scene Edit tool adapter seam. The Annotation module should own pointer start, editor activation, Escape/cancel behavior, persistence commit, and teardown cleanup.

## User Value
Annotation behavior remains unchanged while the router no longer needs Annotation-specific editor state knowledge.

## Out of Scope
Do not redesign Annotation layout, text bounds, hit testing, or persisted Annotation fields.
design:
## Agent Brief
**Category:** task
**Summary:** Move Annotation Text editing behavior behind the internal Scene Edit tool adapter seam.

**Current behavior:**
Annotation Text has a dedicated module, but the router still knows when Annotation editor state blocks global keyboard behavior and how Annotation pointer starts route into editor lifecycle.

**Desired behavior:**
Annotation Text becomes an adapter behind the internal Scene Edit tool seam. The adapter owns pointer start, editor activation, commit, Escape/cancel behavior, editor-state reporting, and cleanup. The router only asks the active adapter whether shared input should be suppressed.

**Key interfaces:**
- Scene Edit tool adapter seam
- Annotation editor lifecycle
- Scene Edit transaction interface
- Canvas runtime tool switching lifecycle

**Out of scope:**
- Changing Annotation layout or bounds
- Changing Annotation hit testing
- Changing persisted Annotation fields
acceptance_criteria:
- [ ] Annotation Text placement, editing, commit, cancellation, and cleanup remain user-equivalent.
- [ ] Annotation editor lifecycle and keyboard suppression are owned behind the Scene Edit tool adapter seam.
- [ ] The router does not contain Annotation-specific editor-state branches beyond adapter registration and dispatch.
- [ ] Focused tests cover editor activation, commit, Escape/cancel, keyboard suppression, tool-switch cleanup, and dispose cleanup.
- [ ] Required frontend typecheck and focused Vitest coverage pass.
close_reason: Moved Text Annotation placement and editor lifecycle behind the Scene Edit tool adapter seam. Router now dispatches text pointer starts through the adapter and asks the active adapter for shared keyboard suppression instead of reading editor state directly. Verified with npm test -- scene-interaction-tool-boundary.test.ts scene-interaction.test.ts -t "Annotation|pending text|tool module boundaries" and npx tsc --noEmit.

title: Create a Timeline Action interaction frame
description:
## Slice Summary
Introduce a deeper Timeline Action interaction frame that owns canvas pan, Timeline Action drag/resize, autoscroll, origin freeze/restore, and commit/abort behavior behind a small Preact-facing seam.

## User Value
Timeline Action dragging stays user-equivalent while the broad canvas workbench starts to shrink into a testable interaction module.

## Out of Scope
Do not move Timeline Action popover orchestration or Target Presentation lifecycle in this slice.
design:
## Agent Brief
**Category:** task
**Summary:** Introduce a deeper Timeline Action interaction frame for drag, resize, pan, and autoscroll.

**Current behavior:**
The Timeline Action canvas workbench protects the render host, but it exposes a wide interface and owns many interaction details directly: drag/resize state, panning, autoscroll, origin freeze/restore, commit/abort behavior, layout cache invalidation, and event ordering.

**Desired behavior:**
A Timeline Action interaction frame owns canvas pan, Timeline Action move/resize, autoscroll, origin freeze/restore, commit/abort behavior, and cleanup behind a smaller Preact-facing seam. The render host remains responsible for canvas mounting and drawing, while the interaction frame owns input ordering.

**Key interfaces:**
- Timeline Action interaction frame
- Timeline Action Workbench
- Planning Projection surface
- Document edit transaction interface

**Out of scope:**
- Moving Timeline Action popover orchestration
- Moving Target Presentation lifecycle
- Changing Timeline Action persisted fields or Planning Projection shape
acceptance_criteria:
- [ ] Timeline Action pan, move, resize, autoscroll, origin freeze/restore, commit, abort, and cleanup remain user-equivalent.
- [ ] Those interaction responsibilities live behind a Timeline Action interaction frame with a smaller Preact-facing seam.
- [ ] The canvas render host no longer needs to know drag/autoscroll/origin-freeze internals.
- [ ] Focused tests cover pan, move, resize, autoscroll, cleanup on unmount, and no-op drag behavior through the interaction frame.
- [ ] Required frontend typecheck and focused Vitest coverage pass.
close_reason: Introduced a Timeline Action interaction frame that owns pan, move, resize, autoscroll, origin freeze/restore, commit/abort, wheel handling, pointer ordering, and drag cleanup behind a smaller canvas-workbench seam. Updated boundary tests and canvas runtime agent docs. Verified with npm test -- timeline-interaction-frame.test.ts timeline-interaction.test.ts timeline-workbench.test.ts interactive-timeline-hover.test.tsx frontend-boundaries-sources.test.ts and npx tsc --noEmit.

title: Deepen Scene Edit tool seam with Plant Spacing
description:
## Slice Summary
Introduce the internal Scene Edit tool adapter seam and migrate Plant Spacing through it as the tracer bullet. The Scene Edit router remains the global listener owner while Plant Spacing owns its snapping, HUD hit checks, drag lifecycle, preview updates, commit/cancel behavior, and cleanup.

## User Value
Plant Spacing keeps the same behavior, but future Scene Edit tools gain a deeper module shape with better locality and test leverage.

## Out of Scope
Do not migrate Object Stamp, Zone, Annotation, or Timeline Action behavior in this slice.
design:
## Agent Brief
**Category:** task
**Summary:** Establish the internal Scene Edit tool adapter seam by moving Plant Spacing behavior behind it.

**Current behavior:**
The Scene Edit router owns global input listeners, but it also knows Plant Spacing-specific rules such as HUD target suppression, source preview timing, drag start thresholds, snapping use, commit/cancel behavior, and cleanup on tool changes.

**Desired behavior:**
The router remains the owner of global listener registration and raw event dispatch. Plant Spacing becomes an adapter behind the internal Scene Edit tool seam and owns its own snapping decisions, HUD interaction checks, preview lifecycle, drag lifecycle, commit/cancel behavior, and cleanup. User-visible Plant Spacing behavior remains equivalent.

**Key interfaces:**
- Scene Edit tool adapter seam
- Scene Edit transaction interface
- Plant Spacing interval and preview behavior
- Canvas runtime tool switching lifecycle

**Out of scope:**
- Moving Object Stamp, Zone, or Annotation behavior
- Changing Plant Spacing semantics, interval persistence, or Placed Plant creation rules
- Changing public canvas command/query/document surfaces
acceptance_criteria:
- [ ] Plant Spacing still previews, commits, cancels, and cleans up with user-equivalent behavior.
- [ ] Plant Spacing owns HUD target checks, source preview timing, drag threshold handling, snapping decisions, and commit/cancel logic behind the Scene Edit tool adapter seam.
- [ ] The Scene Edit router no longer contains Plant Spacing-specific branches beyond adapter registration and dispatch.
- [ ] Focused tests cover Plant Spacing preview, drag commit, Escape/cancel, tool-switch cleanup, and dispose cleanup through the adapter seam.
- [ ] Required frontend typecheck and focused Vitest coverage pass.
close_reason: Moved Plant Spacing behind the Scene Edit tool adapter seam. Router now dispatches through the adapter for Plant Spacing lifecycle, HUD suppression, preview/drag commit, Escape, viewport refresh, and disposal. Verified with npm test -- scene-interaction-tool-boundary.test.ts scene-interaction.test.ts and npx tsc --noEmit.

title: Document the interaction module seam
description:
## Slice Summary
Update agent-facing documentation and boundary tests to record the deepened Scene Edit and Timeline Action interaction seams. The docs should describe router ownership, tool adapter ownership, Timeline Action frame ownership, and the expected testing surface.

## User Value
Future agents can extend Scene Edit and Timeline Action behavior without rediscovering the architecture rules or reintroducing shallow modules.

## Out of Scope
Do not add new interaction behavior in this slice beyond documentation and guardrail tests.
design:
## Agent Brief
**Category:** chore
**Summary:** Document the deepened Scene Edit and Timeline Action interaction seams.

**Current behavior:**
Agent-facing docs describe important canvas and frontend rules, but they do not fully capture the new interaction module ownership after Scene Edit tools and Timeline Action behavior move behind deeper seams.

**Desired behavior:**
Docs and boundary tests record the new ownership model: the Scene Edit router owns global input and dispatch, Scene Edit tool adapters own tool-specific behavior, the Timeline Action interaction frame owns Timeline Action canvas input ordering, and tests should target these interfaces. Future agents should have a durable rule that prevents reintroducing shallow routing modules.

**Key interfaces:**
- Scene Edit router interface
- Scene Edit tool adapter seam
- Timeline Action interaction frame
- Agent-facing frontend and canvas runtime guidance

**Out of scope:**
- Implementing new interaction behavior
- Rewriting unrelated agent docs
- Reopening existing document or map lifecycle decisions
acceptance_criteria:
- [ ] Agent-facing canvas guidance documents Scene Edit router ownership and Scene Edit tool adapter ownership.
- [ ] Agent-facing frontend guidance documents Timeline Action interaction frame ownership and the intended Preact-facing seam.
- [ ] Boundary or source tests guard the new ownership rules where practical.
- [ ] Stale or contradictory instructions are replaced rather than appended as exceptions.
- [ ] Docs-only verification is recorded, or focused tests are run if guardrail tests change.
close_reason: Documented the deepened Scene Edit tool-adapter seam and Timeline Action interaction-frame seam in agent-facing canvas/frontend guides, including ownership and test-surface guidance. Verified docs against guardrails with npm test -- scene-interaction-tool-boundary.test.ts frontend-boundaries-sources.test.ts.

title: Move Zone drawing behind the Scene Edit tool seam
description:
## Slice Summary
Migrate rectangular, elliptical, and polygonal Zone drawing behavior behind the internal Scene Edit tool adapter seam. The Zone module should own draft lifecycle, snapping, measurements, pointer/key handling, commit/cancel behavior, and viewport-dependent refresh.

## User Value
Zone drawing remains user-equivalent while Zone-specific invariants and cleanup get one testable locality.

## Out of Scope
Do not change Zone Measurement display rules, persisted Zone schema, or layer visibility semantics except as needed to preserve existing behavior.
design:
## Agent Brief
**Category:** task
**Summary:** Move Zone drawing behavior behind the internal Scene Edit tool adapter seam.

**Current behavior:**
Zone drawing has a dedicated module, but the router still coordinates Zone-specific pointer and key behavior for rectangular, elliptical, and polygonal Zones, including draft state, snapping, measurement refresh, and draft preservation during panning.

**Desired behavior:**
Zone drawing becomes an adapter behind the internal Scene Edit tool seam. The Zone adapter owns draft lifecycle, snapping, Zone Measurement refresh, pointer/key behavior, commit/cancel behavior, viewport-dependent refresh, and cleanup. The router dispatches raw input and owns shared selection/camera responsibilities.

**Key interfaces:**
- Scene Edit tool adapter seam
- Scene Edit transaction interface
- Zone draft and Zone Measurement behavior
- Canvas runtime viewport refresh lifecycle

**Out of scope:**
- Redesigning Zone Measurement presentation
- Changing persisted Zone fields or Zone Edge geometry
- Changing layer visibility, locking, or opacity semantics
acceptance_criteria:
- [ ] Rectangular, elliptical, and polygonal Zone drawing remain user-equivalent.
- [ ] Zone draft lifecycle, snapping, key handling, commit/cancel behavior, measurement refresh, and cleanup live behind the Scene Edit tool adapter seam.
- [ ] The router does not contain Zone-specific draft, measurement, or polygon key branches beyond adapter registration and dispatch.
- [ ] Focused tests cover rectangle/ellipse creation, polygon draft behavior, Escape/Backspace behavior, panning preservation where applicable, viewport refresh, and dispose cleanup.
- [ ] Required frontend typecheck and focused Vitest coverage pass.
close_reason: Moved rectangle, ellipse, and polygon Zone drawing behind the Scene Edit tool adapter seam. Router now dispatches Zone pointer/key handling, transient cleanup, panning draft preservation, viewport refresh, and selected-Zone measurement refresh through generic adapter hooks. Verified with npm test -- scene-interaction-tool-boundary.test.ts scene-interaction.test.ts and npx tsc --noEmit.

title: Collapse Timeline Action popover and Target Presentation ordering
description:
## Slice Summary
Move Timeline Action popover open/save/delete/cancel ordering, hover/selection cleanup, keyboard delete behavior, tooltip suppression, and Target Presentation writes behind the Timeline Action interaction frame.

## User Value
Interactive Timeline remains user-equivalent while Timeline Action UI state and Target Presentation lifecycle gain one local test surface.

## Out of Scope

title: Lock down the Scene Edit router contract
description:
## Slice Summary
After the major Scene Edit tools sit behind adapters, remove remaining tool-specific routing leakage and add boundary tests that define the router's smaller interface: global listener ownership, camera pan/zoom, selection primitives, raw event dispatch, and shared cleanup.

## User Value
Future Scene Edit work starts from a stable deep module seam instead of expanding the central router.

## Out of Scope
Do not migrate additional design workflows, planning surfaces, or renderer behavior in this slice.
design:
## Agent Brief
**Category:** task
**Summary:** Lock down the smaller Scene Edit router interface after the major tools move behind adapters.

**Current behavior:**
The Scene Edit router owns useful shared behavior, but tool-specific branches still make its interface shallow and invite future Scene Edit behavior to expand the central implementation.

**Desired behavior:**
The router becomes a deeper module with a smaller interface: global listener ownership, raw event capture, shared camera pan/zoom, shared selection primitives, active-tool dispatch, and shared cleanup. Tool-specific snapping, hit-test timing, preview lifecycle, key handling, and cancellation remain behind adapters. Boundary tests guard the import and responsibility rules.

**Key interfaces:**
- Scene Edit router interface
- Scene Edit tool adapter seam
- Canvas runtime command/query/document surfaces
- Scene Edit transaction interface

**Out of scope:**
- Migrating additional planning surfaces
- Changing renderer ownership
- Changing public canvas surfaces
acceptance_criteria:
- [ ] The Scene Edit router owns only global listeners, raw event capture, camera pan/zoom, shared selection primitives, active-tool dispatch, and shared cleanup.
- [ ] Tool-specific snapping, hit-test timing, preview lifecycle, key handling, commit/cancel behavior, and cleanup are owned by adapters.
- [ ] Boundary tests prevent app-facing code from reaching into runtime internals and prevent tool-specific routing leakage from returning.
- [ ] Existing Scene Edit behavior remains user-equivalent across selection, panning, hover, Plant Spacing, Object Stamp, Zone drawing, and Annotation Text.
- [ ] Required frontend typecheck and focused Vitest coverage pass.
close_reason: Locked down the Scene Edit router contract by moving the remaining Plant Stamp placement and species-state cleanup behind a tool adapter and strengthening source boundary tests for all Scene Edit tool adapters. Verified with npm test -- scene-interaction-tool-boundary.test.ts scene-interaction.test.ts, npm test -- frontend-boundaries-sources.test.ts, npm test -- scene-runtime.test.ts -t "resets transient runtime state", and npx tsc --noEmit.

**Current behavior:**
Most saved Location presentation already flows through the Location Workbench, but a few UI surfaces still read Design Session state directly to decide whether a Design exists, whether a Location exists, or whether compass UI should render.

**Desired behavior:**
Saved Location presentation for the Location route, compass readiness, map-layer details, and canvas shell affordances flows through the Location Workbench or a Location-owned read projection. Compass UI may combine saved Location readiness from the Location Workbench with scene-owned north bearing metadata, but it must not read Design Session state directly.

**Key interfaces:**
- Location Workbench saved Location presentation.
- Design Session store as the Location Workbench's internal authority input.
- Canvas and Location UI consumers of saved Location readiness.
- Frontend seam tests that guard Location UI imports.

**Out of scope:**
- Changing Location editing, geocoding, or altitude preservation behavior.
- Moving scene-owned north bearing metadata into the Location Workbench.
- Changing MapLibre lifecycle or terrain behavior.
acceptance_criteria:
- [ ] Remaining saved Location UI consumers use the Location Workbench or a Location-owned read projection instead of importing Design Session state directly.
- [ ] Compass visibility still requires both a saved Location and a non-null north bearing, with scene-owned north bearing kept outside Location authority.
- [ ] Location route empty-state behavior is unchanged when no Design is loaded.
- [ ] Import-boundary tests cover the remaining saved Location consumers that previously escaped the Location Workbench seam.
- [ ] Focused Location/route tests and TypeScript check pass.
close_reason: Moved remaining saved Location UI consumers behind the Location Workbench seam: LocationPanel now gates on useSavedLocationPresentation(), CompassOverlay combines Workbench saved-location readiness with scene-owned northBearingAvailable/northBearingDeg metadata, and boundary tests now cover both consumers. title: Decide Scene Interaction tool-module seam
description:
## Slice Summary
Record the intended internal seam for Scene Interaction tool modules before extracting multiple tool state machines.

## User Value
Maintainers can delegate the extraction slices without each agent inventing a different tool-module shape.

## Out of Scope
Do not implement the extraction in this decision bead. Do not revisit the public SceneCanvasRuntime interface unless the decision explicitly says the current internal seam cannot work.
design:
## Agent Brief
**Category:** decision
**Summary:** Decide the internal Scene Interaction tool-module seam before extraction work begins.

**Current behavior:**
Scene Interaction has a small public interface, but tool-specific state machines share one implementation and one mutable mode model. Extracting tools without a shared decision risks each slice choosing a different internal module shape.

**Desired behavior:**
A maintainer records the internal seam shape for tool modules. The decision should say how tool modules receive shared context, how they own setup/update/teardown, how they interact with Scene Edit transactions, how overlay DOM ownership is handled, and which responsibilities remain in the central interaction router.

**Key interfaces:**
- Scene Interaction public interface.
- Internal tool-module interface.
- Scene Edit transaction interface.
- Overlay lifecycle ownership.
- Camera, snapping, hit testing, and selection context.

**Out of scope:**
- Implementing any extraction.
- Changing user-facing canvas behavior.
- Replacing SceneCanvasRuntime public surfaces.
- Re-litigating existing Scene Edit authority.
acceptance_criteria:
- [ ] The decision records whether tool modules are internal adapters selected by the central Scene Interaction router or another explicit shape.
- [ ] The decision records the shared context each tool module may use, including camera, snapping, Scene Edit, selection, hit testing, overlays, and settings.
- [ ] The decision records setup, update, teardown, and HMR/resource ownership expectations for tool modules.
- [ ] The decision records which first extraction should act as the tracer and why.
- [ ] Downstream extraction beads can be implemented without choosing a new seam shape.
close_reason: Recorded the Scene Interaction tool-module seam in docs/agent/canvas-runtime.md: internal router-selected tool adapters, shared context, lifecycle/resource ownership, Scene Edit expectations, and Annotation Text as the tracer. Verified with git diff --check; app tests skipped because this bead is a docs-only decision.

title: Ignore Plant Spacing HUD pointer moves
description:
## Slice Summary
Prevent pointer movement that originates inside the Plant Spacing HUD from updating the active spacing guide or generated count.

## User Value
A designer can edit the Plant Spacing Interval and move over HUD controls without the canvas guide jumping to HUD coordinates.

## Out of Scope
Do not change Plant Spacing sequence math, interval parsing, pointerdown handling, shortcut behavior, or non-Plant Spacing tool interactions.
design:
## Agent Brief
**Category:** bug
**Summary:** Prevent Plant Spacing HUD-origin pointer movement from changing the active spacing guide.

**Current behavior:**
After a Plant Spacing source is selected, the interaction controller updates the spacing preview from window-level pointer movement. Pointer movement over HUD controls can therefore be interpreted as a canvas endpoint, making the guide and generated count jump while the user is editing the interval or moving across controls.

**Desired behavior:**
Pointer movement that originates inside the Plant Spacing HUD or its controls is treated as HUD interaction only. It must not update the spacing guide endpoint, generated count, ghost preview, or pending commit endpoint. Pointer movement outside the HUD continues to update Plant Spacing previews normally, including click-hold drag previews.

**Key interfaces:**
- Plant Spacing HUD control surface
- Scene interaction pointer movement handling
- Plant Spacing preview endpoint and generated count
- Plant Spacing click-hold drag preview

**Out of scope:**
- Changing Plant Spacing sequence math
- Changing interval parsing or formatting
- Changing pointerdown or keyboard shortcut behavior beyond what is necessary to ignore HUD-origin pointer movement
- Reworking non-Plant Spacing canvas interactions
acceptance_criteria:
- [ ] Moving the pointer over the Plant Spacing HUD after a source is selected does not update the guide endpoint, length label, generated count, or ghost positions.
- [ ] Moving the pointer over the Plant Spacing interval input does not alter the active spacing guide while the user is editing.
- [ ] Canvas pointer movement outside the HUD still updates Plant Spacing preview geometry normally.
- [ ] Click-hold Plant Spacing drag behavior outside the HUD still previews and commits as before.
- [ ] Focused regression tests cover HUD-origin pointermove events and normal canvas pointermove behavior.
close_reason: Fixed Plant Spacing HUD pointermove handling by consuming HUD-origin pointer movement before it reaches the window canvas interaction listener, so interval input/HUD movement no longer changes the active spacing guide while normal canvas movement still works. Tests: npm test -- --run src/__tests__/scene-interaction.test.ts -t 'HUD pointermoves'; npm test -- --run src/__tests__/scene-interaction.test.ts -t 'Plant Spacing'.

title: Stabilize Plant Spacing HUD event boundaries
description:
## Slice Summary
Codify the Plant Spacing HUD as a control surface rather than canvas input by auditing HUD-origin pointer and keyboard events after the known pointermove leak is fixed.

## User Value
A designer can trust that interacting with Plant Spacing controls will not accidentally alter the spacing guide, commit generated plants, or block expected app shortcuts.

## Out of Scope
Do not redesign the Plant Spacing HUD, change Plant Spacing generation math, change shortcut assignments, or refactor unrelated canvas tools.
design:
## Agent Brief
**Category:** bug
**Summary:** Audit and test the Plant Spacing HUD/canvas event boundary as a single invariant.

**Current behavior:**
Recent Plant Spacing fixes have addressed individual HUD/canvas event leaks one at a time, including HUD pointerdowns, interval input keyboard handling, focus behavior, and now HUD pointer movement. This pattern makes it easy for another HUD-origin event to slip through and affect canvas interaction state.

**Desired behavior:**
The Plant Spacing HUD is explicitly protected as a control surface. HUD-origin events that belong to controls must not be interpreted as canvas source sampling, endpoint previewing, endpoint committing, or generated plant creation. Events outside the HUD continue to drive Plant Spacing normally. Existing interval input behavior remains intact: Enter and Escape are handled by the tool, while unrelated global shortcuts can still bubble according to the app command policy.

**Key interfaces:**
- Plant Spacing HUD pointer and keyboard event handling
- Scene interaction pointerdown, pointermove, pointerup, and keyboard handling
- Plant Spacing interval input focus, blur, Enter, Escape, and shortcut bubbling behavior
- Plant Spacing preview and commit endpoint lifecycle

**Out of scope:**
- Redesigning HUD layout or visual styling
- Changing shortcut assignments
- Changing Plant Spacing sequence math, density thresholds, or hard caps
- Refactoring unrelated canvas tools
acceptance_criteria:
- [ ] Tests or implementation-level guards cover HUD-origin pointerdown, pointermove, and relevant control clicks as non-canvas input.
- [ ] Tests preserve interval input keyboard behavior: Enter commits interval input, Escape cancels Plant Spacing, and unrelated global shortcut keydowns can bubble.
- [ ] Tests preserve interval input focus/blur behavior without stealing focus from other controls.
- [ ] Normal canvas-origin source sampling, previewing, two-click commits, and click-hold drag commits still work.
- [ ] Any newly discovered HUD/canvas boundary gaps are fixed in this bead or split into concrete follow-up beads if they exceed this scope.
close_reason: Stabilized the Plant Spacing HUD/canvas event boundary by keeping HUD-origin pointerdown, pointermove, and pointerup events from acting as canvas source/endpoint input while preserving interval keyboard/focus behavior and normal canvas-origin Plant Spacing flows. Tests: npm test -- --run src/__tests__/scene-interaction.test.ts -t 'HUD pointer'; npm test -- --run src/__tests__/scene-interaction.test.ts -t 'Plant Spacing'; npm test -- --run src/__tests__/scene-interaction.test.ts; npx tsc --noEmit; npm test; git diff --check.

title: Refresh Plant Spacing drag endpoint on release
description:
## Slice Summary
Ensure click-hold Plant Spacing drag commits use the pointerup release coordinate when the release represents a newer endpoint than the last pointermove, while still preserving an intentionally Shift-constrained preview.

## User Value
A designer can release a Plant Spacing drag at the intended endpoint and trust the generated plants will follow the final release position instead of stopping at a stale preview.

## Out of Scope
Do not change Plant Spacing sequence math, interval parsing, zero-length guide label formatting, two-click endpoint placement, or non-Plant Spacing drag behavior.
design:
## Agent Brief
**Category:** bug
**Summary:** Refresh Plant Spacing drag commit endpoints from pointerup when the release coordinate is newer than the latest preview.

**Current behavior:**
During click-hold Plant Spacing drag, commit can reuse the last preview endpoint unconditionally. If the pointer is released at a different coordinate than the last pointermove, the generated plants stop at the stale preview endpoint rather than the final release position.

**Desired behavior:**
Plant Spacing drag commit should use the final pointerup coordinate when it represents a newer unconstrained endpoint. If the latest visible preview was intentionally Shift-constrained and the only difference is that Shift was released before pointerup, commit must preserve the constrained preview so generated plants match what the user saw. Normal unconstrained click-hold drag, Shift-constrained click-hold drag, and two-click endpoint placement should keep their existing behavior.

**Key interfaces:**
- Plant Spacing click-hold drag preview
- Plant Spacing pointerup endpoint commit
- Shift direction constraint behavior
- Plant Spacing generated plant placement

**Out of scope:**
- Changing generated plant spacing math
- Changing interval parsing, warnings, hard caps, or zero-length label formatting
- Changing keyboard shortcut behavior
- Changing drag behavior for other canvas tools
acceptance_criteria:
- [ ] Releasing a Plant Spacing click-hold drag at a coordinate that differs from the last pointermove commits plants along the release endpoint.
- [ ] Releasing Shift before pointerup after a Shift-constrained preview still commits plants along the latest visible constrained guide.
- [ ] Unconstrained Plant Spacing click-hold drag still commits the previewed unconstrained guide when pointerup does not move.
- [ ] The normal two-click Plant Spacing endpoint flow still works.
- [ ] Focused regression tests cover both stale release-coordinate refresh and Shift-constrained preservation.
close_reason: Fixed Plant Spacing click-hold drag commits to refresh from the pointerup endpoint when the release coordinate is newer than the preview, while preserving Shift-constrained preview commits when Shift is released at the same coordinate. Tests: npm test -- --run src/__tests__/scene-interaction.test.ts -t 'pointerup endpoint'; npm test -- --run src/__tests__/scene-interaction.test.ts -t 'latest Shift-constrained'; npm test -- --run src/__tests__/scene-interaction.test.ts -t 'Plant Spacing'; npm test -- --run src/__tests__/scene-interaction.test.ts src/__tests__/plant-spacing-interval.test.ts; npx tsc --noEmit; npm test; git diff --check.

title: Preserve Shift-constrained Plant Spacing endpoint on commit
description:
## Slice Summary
Ensure Plant Spacing commits use the same endpoint that was shown in the latest preview, even if the Shift modifier changes before pointerup.

## User Value
Users can trust that the generated plants match the visible Shift-constrained spacing guide they previewed during a click-hold drag.

## Out of Scope
Do not change Plant Spacing sequence math, interval validity, shortcut behavior, or non-Plant Spacing drag interactions.
design:
## Agent Brief
**Category:** bug
**Summary:** Preserve the last previewed Shift-constrained Plant Spacing endpoint when committing a drag.

**Current behavior:**
During Plant Spacing click-hold drag, the live preview endpoint can be constrained by Shift. If the user releases Shift before pointerup without moving the pointer again, the commit path can recompute the endpoint from the pointerup event without Shift. The generated plants then follow a different line than the guide the user saw.

**Desired behavior:**
Plant Spacing drag commit should use the same endpoint represented by the latest visible preview. Modifier changes between the last preview update and pointerup must not make the committed generated plants diverge from the visible guide. Normal unconstrained drag, Shift-constrained drag, and two-click endpoint placement should keep their existing behavior.

**Key interfaces:**
- Plant Spacing click-hold drag preview
- Plant Spacing endpoint commit on pointerup
- Shift direction constraint behavior
- Plant Spacing generated plant placement

**Out of scope:**
- Changing generated plant spacing math
- Changing interval parsing, warnings, or hard cap behavior
- Changing keyboard shortcut behavior
- Changing drag behavior for other canvas tools
acceptance_criteria:
- [ ] Releasing Shift before pointerup after a Shift-constrained Plant Spacing drag commits plants along the same guide shown in the preview.
- [ ] The latest previewed endpoint is preserved for Plant Spacing drag commit instead of recomputing from stale modifier state.
- [ ] Unconstrained Plant Spacing drag still commits the previewed unconstrained guide.
- [ ] The normal two-click Plant Spacing endpoint flow still works.
- [ ] Focused regression tests cover Shift held during preview then released before pointerup.
close_reason: Fixed Plant Spacing click-hold drag commit to reuse the latest preview endpoint, so releasing Shift before pointerup preserves the visible constrained guide. Tests: npm test -- --run src/__tests__/scene-interaction.test.ts -t 'latest Shift-constrained'; npm test -- --run src/__tests__/scene-interaction.test.ts -t 'Plant Spacing'; npm test -- --run src/__tests__/scene-interaction.test.ts; npx tsc --noEmit; npm test; git diff --check.

title: Let Plant Spacing interval input pass global shortcuts
description:
## Slice Summary
Prevent the Plant Spacing interval input from swallowing global app shortcuts while still handling its own Enter and Escape behavior.

## User Value
Users can save, open the command palette, and use other app-level shortcuts even when the Plant Spacing interval input has focus.

## Out of Scope
Do not change interval parsing, text editing behavior, app command registry shortcuts, or non-Plant Spacing inputs.
design:
## Agent Brief
**Category:** bug
**Summary:** Let global app shortcuts pass through the Plant Spacing interval input unless the input is handling Enter or Escape.

**Current behavior:**
The Plant Spacing interval input stops propagation for every keydown. That prevents app-level shortcut handling from seeing global shortcuts while the input has focus, including shortcuts that are intentionally allowed from editable targets.

**Desired behavior:**
The interval input should consume only keys it owns, specifically Enter for interval commit and Escape for Plant Spacing cancellation. Other keydown events, including Ctrl/Cmd and command-palette shortcuts, should be allowed to bubble so the app command registry can handle them according to its existing editable-target policy.

**Key interfaces:**
- Plant Spacing interval input keyboard handling
- App command registry global shortcut handling
- Editable-target shortcut policy

**Out of scope:**
- Changing the global shortcut registry
- Changing interval parsing or commit behavior
- Changing keyboard behavior for non-Plant Spacing inputs
acceptance_criteria:
- [ ] Enter in the Plant Spacing interval input still commits the interval and does not leak to unrelated canvas keyboard behavior.
- [ ] Escape in the Plant Spacing interval input still cancels the Plant Spacing source/flow.
- [ ] Non-Enter/Escape keydown events in the interval input are not stopped by the HUD input handler.
- [ ] A focused regression test proves a representative global shortcut keydown can bubble from the interval input.
- [ ] Existing Plant Spacing interval input tests continue to pass.
close_reason: Changed the Plant Spacing interval input to stop propagation only for Enter and Escape, allowing global shortcut keydowns to bubble while preserving interval commit/cancel behavior. Tests: npm test -- --run src/__tests__/scene-interaction.test.ts -t lets\ Plant\ Spacing\ interval\ input\ bubble\ global\ shortcut\ keys; npm test -- --run src/__tests__/scene-interaction.test.ts -t Plant\ Spacing\ interval; npm test -- --run src/__tests__/scene-interaction.test.ts -t Plant\ Spacing; npm test -- --run src/__tests__/scene-interaction.test.ts; npx tsc --noEmit; npm test; git diff --check.

title: Require intentional Plant Spacing drag before fast-path commit
description:
## Slice Summary
Prevent minor pointer jitter after Plant Spacing source sampling from entering the click-hold drag commit path and creating plants unexpectedly.

## User Value
A normal click to sample a source placed plant cannot accidentally generate plants because of one-pixel pointer movement, while intentional click-hold dragging still works.

## Out of Scope
Do not change two-click Plant Spacing endpoint placement, spacing interval validity, generated plant math, or unrelated canvas drag thresholds.
design:
## Agent Brief
**Category:** bug
**Summary:** Require a real intentional Plant Spacing click-hold drag before using the pointerup fast-path commit.

**Current behavior:**
After a source placed plant is sampled, a very small pointer movement can enter the Plant Spacing drag state. On pointerup, that drag state can commit generated plants if the jittered world endpoint is at least one interval away, which can happen at low zoom or with small intervals.

**Desired behavior:**
Plant Spacing should only use the click-hold drag commit path after an intentional drag gesture. Minor pointer jitter after a source-sampling click should not create generated plants. The normal two-click flow, where the user samples a source and then clicks an endpoint, should keep working.

**Key interfaces:**
- Plant Spacing source sampling
- Plant Spacing click-hold drag gesture detection
- Plant Spacing endpoint commit on pointerup
- Canvas pointer slop/drag threshold behavior

**Out of scope:**
- Changing generated plant spacing math
- Changing the two-click endpoint placement flow
- Changing drag thresholds for unrelated canvas tools unless a shared helper already owns the policy
- Changing interval validity or commit safety cap behavior
acceptance_criteria:
- [ ] A source-sampling click followed by minor pointer jitter does not create generated plants on pointerup.
- [ ] Intentional click-hold Plant Spacing drag still previews and commits generated plants.
- [ ] The normal two-click Plant Spacing flow still samples a source on the first click and commits an endpoint on the second click.
- [ ] Focused regression tests cover minor pointer jitter and intentional drag behavior.
- [ ] Existing Plant Spacing interaction tests continue to pass.
close_reason: Raised Plant Spacing click-hold drag slop to require intentional movement, kept below-threshold movement as preview-only, and prevented minor source-click jitter from creating plants on pointerup while preserving intentional click-hold and two-click flows. Tests: npm test -- --run src/__tests__/scene-interaction.test.ts -t does\ not\ commit\ Plant\ Spacing\ from\ minor\ source-click\ pointer\ jitter; npm test -- --run src/__tests__/scene-interaction.test.ts -t click-hold; npm test -- --run src/__tests__/scene-interaction.test.ts -t Plant\ Spacing; npm test -- --run src/__tests__/scene-interaction.test.ts; npx tsc --noEmit; npm test; git diff --check.

title: Bound Plant Spacing commits before materializing
description:
## Slice Summary
Prevent Plant Spacing from materializing an unbounded number of generated plants when committing a line with a very small but physically valid interval.

## User Value
Users can still enter small agroecological spacing intervals, but a single commit cannot hang the app by allocating hundreds of thousands or millions of plants.

## Out of Scope
Do not change interval validity rules, preview styling, warning threshold color behavior, or unrelated Plant Spacing interaction flows.
design:
## Agent Brief
**Category:** bug
**Summary:** Add a hard Plant Spacing commit safety cap before generated positions are materialized.

**Current behavior:**
Plant Spacing preview generation is capped, but the final commit path recomputes and materializes the full generated position list. With a tiny but valid interval over a normal-length line, the app can allocate a very large plant list and hang during the scene edit.

**Desired behavior:**
Before committing Plant Spacing output, compute or otherwise bound the generated count without materializing an unsafe number of positions. If the count exceeds the hard safety cap, do not create plants and show a clear message that the line is too dense to commit. Physically valid intervals should remain valid inputs; the safety cap protects only the one-shot commit size.

Use a hard cap of 5,000 generated plants per Plant Spacing commit. Keep the existing 100-plant warning threshold as warning-only behavior. Counts from 101 through 5,000 remain allowed; counts above 5,000 are blocked from commit.

**Key interfaces:**
- Plant Spacing line endpoint commit flow
- Plant Spacing generated-count calculation
- Plant Spacing HUD status or error messaging
- Scene edit transaction that appends generated plants

**Out of scope:**
- Changing interval parsing or the 50 cm fallback
- Reintroducing confirmation UX for dense spacing
- Changing the 100-plant warning threshold behavior except as needed to distinguish it from the hard safety cap
- Changing generated plant placement math for safe counts
acceptance_criteria:
- [ ] Plant Spacing commits never materialize more than 5,000 generated plants in one operation.
- [ ] Counts from 101 through 5,000 remain physically valid and commit without a confirmation dialog.
- [ ] Counts above 5,000 are blocked with a clear HUD-visible safety message instead of creating plants.
- [ ] The commit path computes or bounds the generated count before allocating the full generated position list.
- [ ] Regression tests cover a tiny valid interval that would exceed the hard cap and a dense-but-allowed interval below the hard cap.
- [ ] Existing preview cap and 100-plant warning behavior remain coherent with the new hard commit cap.
close_reason: Added a 5,000 generated-plant hard safety cap to Plant Spacing commits before full position materialization, kept 101 through 5,000 generated plants warning-only and directly committable, surfaced blocked counts in the HUD with localized guidance, and added regression coverage for over-cap blocking plus exactly-cap commit. Tests: npm test -- --run src/__tests__/scene-interaction.test.ts -t blocks\ Plant\ Spacing\ commits\ above\ the\ hard\ safety\ cap; npm test -- --run src/__tests__/scene-interaction.test.ts -t hard\ safety\ cap; npm test -- --run src/__tests__/scene-interaction.test.ts -t Plant\ Spacing; npm test -- --run src/__tests__/scene-interaction.test.ts; npx tsc --noEmit; npm test; git diff --check.

title: Do not steal focus on Plant Spacing interval blur
description:
## Slice Summary
Prevent the Plant Spacing Interval input from moving focus back to the canvas during ordinary blur.

## User Value
Keyboard and pointer users can tab or click away from the Plant Spacing HUD without focus being immediately stolen by the canvas.

## Out of Scope
Do not change Plant Spacing interval parsing, Enter commit semantics, HUD layout, spacing sequence math, or behavior for other canvas tool inputs.
design:
## Agent Brief
**Category:** bug
**Summary:** Keep Plant Spacing Interval blur from stealing focus back to the canvas.

**Current behavior:**
The Plant Spacing Interval input uses the same commit path for Enter and blur. That commit path intentionally focuses the canvas after valid interval commits. When the input blurs because the user tabs or clicks somewhere else, focus is pulled back to the canvas, trapping keyboard users in the HUD and preventing clicked controls from retaining focus.

**Desired behavior:**
Ordinary blur from the Plant Spacing Interval input should persist or validate the interval as appropriate without forcing focus back to the canvas. Pressing Enter should keep the existing intentional commit behavior, including returning focus to the canvas. Invalid interval handling should continue to show invalid state and keep the input usable.

**Key interfaces:**
- Plant Spacing Tool HUD
- Plant Spacing Interval input keyboard and blur handling
- Canvas focus management
- Plant Spacing interval persistence and preview refresh

**Out of scope:**
- Changing Plant Spacing interval parsing or fallback behavior
- Changing Enter commit semantics
- Changing HUD copy or layout
- Changing keyboard behavior for other canvas tools
acceptance_criteria:
- [ ] Blurring a valid Plant Spacing Interval input does not move focus back to the canvas.
- [ ] Blurring a valid Plant Spacing Interval input still persists or applies the interval consistently with existing blur behavior.
- [ ] Pressing Enter in the Plant Spacing Interval input still commits the interval and returns focus to the canvas.
- [ ] Blurring or pressing Enter with an invalid interval continues to show invalid state without creating plants.
- [ ] Focused regression tests cover blur focus behavior and preserve Enter commit behavior.
close_reason: Separated Plant Spacing interval blur from Enter commit so valid/invalid blur applies state without stealing focus, while Enter still returns focus to the canvas. Tests: npm test -- --run src/__tests__/scene-interaction.test.ts -t Plant\ Spacing\ interval; npm test -- --run src/__tests__/scene-interaction.test.ts; npx tsc --noEmit; npm test.

title: Revalidate Plant Spacing source before commit
description:
## Slice Summary
Prevent Plant Spacing from committing generated plants when the sampled source or plants layer becomes unavailable after source picking.

## User Value
A designer cannot accidentally mutate a locked or hidden plant layer by sampling a plant, changing layer state, and then clicking a spacing endpoint.

## Out of Scope
Do not change Plant Spacing sequence math, HUD layout, dense-count behavior, or layer panel behavior.
design:
## Agent Brief
**Category:** bug
**Summary:** Revalidate the live Plant Spacing source and plant layer before committing generated plants.

**Current behavior:**
Plant Spacing samples a placed plant into a transient source. If that source plant or the plants layer becomes hidden or locked before the endpoint click, the commit path can still append generated plants from the stale sampled clone.

**Desired behavior:**
Plant Spacing must recheck that the sampled source is still a live, visible, unlocked placed plant on a visible, unlocked plants layer immediately before committing generated plants. If the source is no longer committable, the tool must not mutate the scene. The source/preview state should recover coherently instead of leaving the user in a misleading placement state.

**Key interfaces:**
- Plant Spacing source sampling and endpoint commit interaction
- Scene layer visibility and lock state
- Scene edit transaction for generated placed plants
- Selection after Plant Spacing commit

**Out of scope:**
- Changing spacing sequence math
- Changing dense generated-count behavior
- Changing layer panel behavior
- Changing Object Stamp behavior except as a reference for the same guard semantics
acceptance_criteria:
- [ ] Plant Spacing does not commit generated plants if the sampled source plant becomes locked before endpoint commit.
- [ ] Plant Spacing does not commit generated plants if the sampled source plant becomes unavailable or hidden before endpoint commit.
- [ ] Plant Spacing does not commit generated plants if the plants layer becomes locked before endpoint commit.
- [ ] Plant Spacing does not commit generated plants if the plants layer becomes hidden before endpoint commit.
- [ ] When commit is blocked by source/layer freshness, the tool leaves no generated plants and presents a coherent source-picking or missed-source state.
- [ ] Focused regression tests cover source lock/removal or layer lock/visibility changes between source pick and endpoint commit.
close_reason: Revalidated the live Plant Spacing source and plants layer before commit, clearing stale placement state without mutating when the source is locked, removed, or the plants layer is locked/hidden. Tests: npm test -- --run src/__tests__/scene-interaction.test.ts; npx tsc --noEmit; npm test.

title: Size Plant Spacing preview ghosts from plant presentation
description:
## Slice Summary
Make Plant Spacing preview ghosts match the footprint that generated plants will render with the current plant presentation settings.

## User Value
A designer using canopy-size presentation can trust the preview row to show the actual committed plant footprint before placing the sequence.

## Out of Scope
Do not change Plant Spacing sequence math, committed plant rendering, plant presentation mode semantics, or generated plant geometry.
design:
## Agent Brief
**Category:** bug
**Summary:** Render Plant Spacing preview ghosts with the same plant presentation footprint used by committed plants.

**Current behavior:**
Plant Spacing preview ghosts render as fixed 12px dots. In canopy-size presentation mode, committed plants render from their plant presentation radius, so the preview can materially understate the row footprint and hide canopy overlaps.

**Desired behavior:**
Plant Spacing preview ghosts should be sized from the same plant presentation context that committed generated plants will use for rendering. In canopy-size mode, preview ghosts should visually match the canopy footprint for the sampled plant. In default presentation, preview ghosts should continue to use the existing default dot footprint semantics.

**Key interfaces:**
- Plant Spacing preview overlay
- Plant presentation context and footprint/radius helpers
- Plant size mode rendering
- Viewport/camera scaling for screen-space preview overlays

**Out of scope:**
- Changing committed plant rendering
- Changing Plant Spacing sequence math
- Changing size mode semantics
- Adding new preview controls or warnings
acceptance_criteria:
- [ ] Plant Spacing preview ghosts in canopy-size mode use the sampled plant's presentation footprint rather than a fixed 12px dot.
- [ ] Plant Spacing preview ghosts in default-size mode remain visually consistent with current default plant dot presentation.
- [ ] Preview ghost footprint updates correctly when viewport scale changes.
- [ ] Focused regression tests cover canopy-size preview footprint sizing before commit.
- [ ] Existing Plant Spacing preview count, guide, length label, and direct commit behavior continue to pass.
close_reason: Sized Plant Spacing preview ghosts from the sampled plant presentation footprint, including canopy mode and viewport refresh coverage. Tests: npm test -- --run src/__tests__/scene-interaction.test.ts -t Plant\ Spacing; npm test -- --run src/__tests__/scene-interaction.test.ts; npx tsc --noEmit; npm test.

title: Restyle Plant Spacing HUD as a compact tool surface
description:
## Slice Summary
Restyle the Plant Spacing Tool HUD so it reads as one compact, coherent canvas tool surface instead of separate labels, warnings, and confirmation controls.

## User Value
A designer using Plant Spacing sees the sampled placed plant, interval input, generated count, and Escape hint without redundant tool/status copy or blocking dense-count confirmation friction.

## Out of Scope
Do not change Plant Spacing sequence math, the Plant Spacing shortcut, the dense threshold value, or Canvas Notice Layout placement.
design:
## Agent Brief
**Category:** feature
**Summary:** Restyle the Plant Spacing Tool HUD into a compact warning-only tool surface.

**Current behavior:**
The Plant Spacing Tool HUD repeats the tool name and generic selected-state copy, shows a visible Cancel button, uses separate dense warning copy, and can show a Confirm/Cancel flow when the generated placed-plant count crosses the dense threshold.

**Desired behavior:**
The Plant Spacing Tool HUD should follow the documented design-system decisions: before a source is sampled, show the source-picking instruction with a muted `Esc to exit` hint. After a placed plant is sampled, make the sampled plant name the primary line, followed by the Plant Spacing Interval input row, a single generated-count line, and a muted `Esc to cancel` hint. Remove the visible Cancel button, remove `Plant Spacing` and `Source selected` copy from the HUD, remove dense Confirm/Cancel controls, and remove separate dense-warning sentence copy. Dense counts above 100 remain physically valid and commit directly; only the generated-count line changes emphasis using the UI accent color and stronger weight.

**Key interfaces:**
- Plant Spacing Tool HUD
- Plant Spacing Interval input
- Generated placed-plant count presentation
- Plant Spacing dense threshold behavior
- Canvas Tool HUD design-system guidance

**Out of scope:**
- Changing Plant Spacing sequence math
- Changing the 100 generated-plant dense threshold value
- Changing Plant Spacing shortcut assignment
- Changing Canvas Notice Layout placement
- Adding a replacement modal or blocking dense-count prompt elsewhere
acceptance_criteria:
- [ ] The Plant Spacing HUD no longer shows `Plant Spacing` as an in-HUD title.
- [ ] The Plant Spacing HUD no longer shows `Source selected` after sampling a placed plant.
- [ ] After sampling, the sampled placed plant name is the primary HUD line.
- [ ] The HUD shows a compact stack with the Plant Spacing Interval input, one generated-count line, and an Escape hint.
- [ ] The visible Cancel button is removed; source-picking shows `Esc to exit`, and source-selected state shows `Esc to cancel`.
- [ ] Dense generated counts above 100 do not show Confirm/Cancel controls or a separate dense-warning sentence.
- [ ] Dense generated counts above 100 remain directly committable and use the UI accent color/weight on the single generated-count line.
- [ ] Focused tests cover source-picking copy, source-selected copy, dense count styling/behavior, and direct dense commit without confirmation.
close_reason: Restyled Plant Spacing HUD as a compact Escape-hint tool surface, removed dense confirmation controls, and verified direct dense commits with scene interaction coverage. Tests: npm test -- --run src/__tests__/scene-interaction.test.ts; npx tsc --noEmit; npm test.

title: Handle Escape from Plant Spacing interval input
description:
## Slice Summary
Restore Plant Spacing keyboard cancellation while the Plant Spacing Interval input has focus after source sampling.

## User Value
A designer can press Escape to cancel Plant Spacing immediately after sampling a placed plant, without needing to blur the interval input first.

## Out of Scope
Do not change Plant Spacing Interval parsing, Enter commit behavior, dense confirmation behavior, or keyboard behavior for other canvas tools.
design:
## Agent Brief
**Category:** bug
**Summary:** Make Escape cancel Plant Spacing even when the Plant Spacing Interval input is focused.

**Current behavior:**
After Plant Spacing samples a source placed plant, the Plant Spacing Interval input receives focus. Its key handler stops propagation for handled and unhandled keys, but only commits Enter. Escape is swallowed by the input and never reaches the canvas interaction controller, so keyboard cancellation does nothing until focus leaves the input.

**Desired behavior:**
Pressing Escape while the Plant Spacing Interval input is focused should cancel Plant Spacing in the same way Escape does from the canvas. Enter should keep committing the interval input as before. Other input typing behavior should remain unchanged and should not leak ordinary text-entry keys to canvas shortcuts.

**Key interfaces:**
- Plant Spacing Tool HUD
- Plant Spacing Interval input
- Canvas keyboard interaction handling
- Plant Spacing cancel behavior

**Out of scope:**
- Changing Plant Spacing Interval parsing or fallback behavior
- Changing dense confirmation behavior
- Changing the Plant Spacing shortcut
- Changing keyboard behavior for other canvas tools
acceptance_criteria:
- [ ] Pressing Escape while the Plant Spacing Interval input is focused cancels Plant Spacing or returns it to the expected source-picking/canvas state.
- [ ] Pressing Enter in the Plant Spacing Interval input still commits a valid interval as before.
- [ ] Ordinary text-entry keys in the interval input are not routed to canvas shortcuts.
- [ ] Escape behavior from the canvas outside the interval input remains unchanged.
- [ ] Focused SceneInteraction or Plant Spacing overlay tests cover Escape from the focused interval input.
close_reason: Handled Escape from the focused Plant Spacing Interval input through the existing Plant Spacing cancel path, added focused SceneInteraction regression coverage, and verified with the full SceneInteraction test file plus npx tsc --noEmit.

title: Stabilize dense Plant Spacing confirmation
description:
## Slice Summary
Keep dense Plant Spacing confirmation modal state synchronized with the spacing guide and Plant Spacing Interval that the user sees.

## User Value
A designer reviewing a dense Plant Spacing confirmation cannot accidentally bypass it with canvas clicks, and Confirm commits the same generated placed plants represented by the HUD.

## Out of Scope
Do not change the dense threshold, add new confirmation policies, or change normal Plant Spacing commits at or below the dense threshold.
design:
## Agent Brief
**Category:** bug
**Summary:** Keep dense Plant Spacing confirmation state synchronized with the visible preview and prevent canvas clicks from bypassing it.

**Current behavior:**
After dense Plant Spacing confirmation is shown, subsequent canvas pointerdown events can be treated as new endpoint commits while confirmation is still pending. The Plant Spacing Interval can also remain editable in a way that updates the visible preview/count while Confirm still commits the previously frozen generated positions.

**Desired behavior:**
While dense confirmation is pending, canvas clicks must not overwrite or bypass the pending confirmation. If the Plant Spacing Interval changes during confirmation, confirmation must be cleared or rebuilt so Confirm can only commit positions that match the currently visible preview and count.

**Key interfaces:**
- Plant Spacing dense confirmation state
- Plant Spacing Interval editing
- Canvas pointer interaction handling
- Plant Spacing generated positions and visible preview/count

**Out of scope:**
- Changing the dense threshold
- Adding a new confirmation policy for non-dense Plant Spacing commits
- Changing saved Plant Spacing Interval persistence semantics except where needed to keep confirmation synchronized
- Changing object selection or undo behavior beyond preserving existing Plant Spacing commit semantics
acceptance_criteria:
- [ ] Canvas clicks while dense confirmation is pending do not commit a new endpoint, overwrite the pending confirmation, or bypass the confirmation.
- [ ] Changing the Plant Spacing Interval while dense confirmation is pending clears or recomputes confirmation so Confirm cannot commit stale generated positions.
- [ ] Confirm commits generated placed plants that match the currently visible Plant Spacing count and spacing guide.
- [ ] Cancel returns to a live preview state without stale confirmation data.
- [ ] Focused tests cover pending-confirmation canvas clicks and interval changes during confirmation.
close_reason: Kept dense Plant Spacing confirmation stable by ignoring canvas endpoint clicks while confirmation is pending and clearing stale confirmation when the interval changes, with focused SceneInteraction coverage and npx tsc --noEmit passing.

title: Hide missing-location canvas notice
description:
## Slice Summary
Stop rendering a canvas Location Notice when map/site layers are visible but the Design has no Location.

## User Value
A designer can sketch without a site without being nagged by persistent canvas chrome. Location Notices remain focused on active map/site context once a Location exists.

## Out of Scope
Do not change the Location Workbench, map layer toggles, location persistence, geocoding, or add a replacement prompt elsewhere.
design:
## Agent Brief
**Category:** bug
**Summary:** Hide canvas Location Notices when the Design has no Location.

**Current behavior:**
When map/site layers are visible and the Design has no Location, the canvas renders a passive Location Notice that says the user must set a design location first.

**Desired behavior:**
When the Design has no Location, the canvas does not render a Location Notice, even if map/site layers are visible. Location Notices still render in the bottom-left safe slot for loading, ready, precision warning, and map/terrain error states after a Location exists.

**Key interfaces:**
- Location Notice presentation
- Site/map readiness feedback
- Location Workbench saved-location state
- Canvas Notice Layout bottom-left safe slot

**Out of scope:**
- Changing the Location Workbench
- Changing map layer toggles or basemap visibility settings
- Changing location persistence or geocoding behavior
- Adding a replacement missing-location prompt elsewhere
acceptance_criteria:
- [ ] No canvas `role="status"` Location Notice is rendered when the Design has no Location, even if map/site layers are visible.
- [ ] Loading, ready, map error, terrain error, and precision warning Location Notices still render in the bottom-left safe slot when a Location exists.
- [ ] Existing Location Workbench and map/layer controls are unchanged.
- [ ] Focused CanvasPanel tests cover no-location hidden behavior and location-present status behavior.
- [ ] Design-system or agent docs are updated if they currently imply missing-location notices render on the canvas.
close_reason: Hid canvas Location Notices when a Design has no Location, kept location-present loading/ready/error/precision notices in the bottom-left safe slot, updated Location Notice docs, and verified with focused CanvasPanel Vitest, full frontend Vitest, TypeScript, and diff check.

title: Add compact fallback for constrained canvas notices
description:
## Slice Summary
Add constrained-layout behavior so Tool HUDs and Location Notices remain visible on small or crowded canvas sizes while preserving priority between interaction-critical and informational messages.

## User Value
A designer on a small canvas or with dense chrome still sees the Plant Spacing primary instruction and the shortest useful Location Notice status instead of clipped, hidden, or overlapping messages.

## Out of Scope
Do not add scene-aware avoidance of placed plants, zones, or guides. Do not introduce new notice types beyond Tool HUD and Location Notice behavior.
design:
## Agent Brief
**Category:** bug
**Summary:** Keep canvas notices visible under constrained canvas sizes.

**Current behavior:**
Canvas notices can overlap fixed chrome or become unreadable when the canvas is small or the bottom-left area is occupied by the scale bar.

**Desired behavior:**
Canvas notices degrade predictably under layout pressure. Active Tool HUDs keep their primary instruction visible and have priority over Location Notices. Location Notices prefer bottom-left above the scale bar, then shift to the right of the scale bar when vertical space is tight, then compact to a one-line status with ellipsis while keeping the status dot and shortest useful label visible.

**Key interfaces:**
- Canvas Notice Layout constrained placement
- Tool HUD priority behavior
- Location Notice compact presentation
- Ruler and scale-bar reserved chrome

**Out of scope:**
- Avoiding arbitrary design objects such as placed plants, zones, annotations, or guides
- Adding new notice categories
- Changing Location Workbench behavior
- Changing Plant Spacing generation behavior
acceptance_criteria:
- [ ] Active Tool HUDs keep their primary instruction visible on constrained canvas sizes.
- [ ] Location Notices prefer bottom-left above the scale bar, then shift right of the scale bar when vertical space is tight.
- [ ] Location Notices compact to one line with ellipsis before disappearing or clipping.
- [ ] The status dot and shortest useful Location Notice label remain visible under severe layout pressure.
- [ ] Focused tests cover constrained layout behavior for both an active Tool HUD and a Location Notice.
close_reason: Added constrained Canvas Notice Layout fallback behavior: Location Notices shift right of the scale bar or compact under severe pressure, Plant Spacing HUDs expose compact widths, and focused layout/CanvasPanel/SceneInteraction tests verify the behavior.

title: Move Location Notices to bottom-left
description:
## Slice Summary
Move the full site/map readiness feedback family into the bottom-left Location Notice safe slot and keep it separate from active Tool HUD guidance.

## User Value
A designer can see missing-location, loading, ready, precision warning, and map/terrain error states without those notices competing with active tool instructions in the top-left canvas area.

## Out of Scope
Do not make the notice clickable. Do not change the Location Workbench. Do not change when map/site readiness feedback appears.
design:
## Agent Brief
**Category:** bug
**Summary:** Move site/map readiness feedback into the bottom-left Location Notice safe slot.

**Current behavior:**
Site/map readiness feedback is shown near the top-left canvas area. It can collide with active Tool HUDs such as Plant Spacing and with ruler chrome.

**Desired behavior:**
Missing location, map loading, ready location summary, precision warning, and map/terrain error states are presented as Location Notices in the bottom-left safe slot. The notice remains passive status feedback and stays visually attached to the canvas workspace. It avoids ruler chrome and the scale bar at normal canvas sizes.

**Key interfaces:**
- Location Notice presentation
- Site/map readiness feedback text and tone
- Canvas Notice Layout bottom-left safe slot
- Ruler and scale-bar reserved chrome

**Out of scope:**
- Changing when map/site readiness feedback appears
- Making the Location Notice clickable or opening the Location Workbench
- Changing location persistence, geocoding, or map layer behavior
- Moving active Tool HUDs in this slice
acceptance_criteria:
- [ ] Missing-location feedback appears as a bottom-left Location Notice.
- [ ] Loading, ready, precision warning, and map/terrain error states use the same Location Notice zone and do not jump to top-left.
- [ ] The Location Notice remains passive status feedback with appropriate status semantics.
- [ ] At normal canvas sizes, the Location Notice avoids the scale bar and visible ruler chrome.
- [ ] Focused UI tests cover the bottom-left placement and at least the missing-location state.
close_reason: Moved map/site readiness feedback into the shared bottom-left Location Notice slot above the scale bar while preserving passive status semantics; verified missing-location and loading states with focused CanvasPanel tests.

title: Keep Plant Spacing HUD clear of rulers
description:
## Slice Summary
Move the Plant Spacing active Tool HUD into the top-left canvas safe slot so its instructions and controls remain readable when rulers and other canvas chrome are visible.

## User Value
A designer using Plant Spacing can always read the current instruction, interval input, generated count, and dense confirmation without the HUD being hidden by rulers or Location Notices.

## Out of Scope
Do not change Plant Spacing generation behavior, interval parsing, dense threshold behavior, or shortcut assignment.
design:
## Agent Brief
**Category:** bug
**Summary:** Keep the Plant Spacing Tool HUD readable by placing it in the top-left safe canvas slot.

**Current behavior:**
The Plant Spacing HUD can appear at the raw upper-left canvas edge, where it overlaps or is obscured by the horizontal ruler, vertical ruler, and site/map readiness feedback.

**Desired behavior:**
When Plant Spacing is active, its Tool HUD uses the top-left safe slot from Canvas Notice Layout. The HUD remains clearly visible through source picking, source selected, interval input, live preview, invalid interval, and dense confirmation states. It should not overlap ruler chrome and should have priority over informational Location Notices.

**Key interfaces:**
- Plant Spacing Tool HUD presentation
- Canvas Notice Layout top-left safe slot
- Ruler and scale-bar reserved chrome
- Keyboard and pointer behavior for Plant Spacing controls

**Out of scope:**
- Changing Plant Spacing sequence generation
- Changing interval parsing, fallback, or persisted settings
- Changing the dense-count threshold or confirmation behavior
- Changing the Plant Spacing shortcut
acceptance_criteria:
- [ ] The Plant Spacing HUD appears in the top-left safe slot rather than at the raw canvas edge.
- [ ] The HUD does not overlap the horizontal ruler, vertical ruler, or ruler corner when rulers are visible.
- [ ] Source-picking, source-selected, interval input, invalid interval, preview, and dense confirmation states remain readable.
- [ ] Plant Spacing interactions and existing Plant Spacing tests continue to pass.
- [ ] Focused tests cover HUD placement with rulers visible.
close_reason: Moved the Plant Spacing Tool HUD onto the shared top-left safe slot so it clears ruler chrome, and verified with focused SceneInteractionController coverage.

title: Define Canvas Notice Layout slots
description:
## Slice Summary
Establish a shared canvas notice placement model so overlays reserve ruler chrome and can use predictable top-left and bottom-left safe slots.

## User Value
Future canvas notices can be placed consistently, and the Plant Spacing HUD and Location Notice fixes can share one layout contract instead of independent offsets.

## Out of Scope
Do not move every existing overlay. Do not make Location Notices interactive. Do not change design objects or scene persistence.
design:
## Agent Brief
**Category:** task
**Summary:** Define a shared Canvas Notice Layout contract for safe canvas overlay placement.

**Current behavior:**
Canvas overlays choose positions independently. Active Tool HUDs, Location Notices, rulers, and the scale bar can compete for the same corner because there is no shared placement model.

**Desired behavior:**
The app has a reusable Canvas Notice Layout contract with a top-left safe slot for active Tool HUDs and a bottom-left safe slot for Location Notices. The contract reserves ruler chrome, including the horizontal ruler, vertical ruler, ruler corner, and scale bar. It establishes priority between interaction-critical Tool HUDs and informational Location Notices without changing scene data or design objects.

**Key interfaces:**
- Canvas overlay placement and screen-space chrome
- Ruler and scale bar visibility/metrics
- Active Tool HUD presentation
- Location Notice presentation

**Out of scope:**
- Moving every existing overlay in this slice
- Making Location Notices interactive
- Avoiding arbitrary placed plants, zones, annotations, or guides
- Changing scene persistence or undo/redo behavior
acceptance_criteria:
- [ ] A shared Canvas Notice Layout placement contract exists for top-left Tool HUDs and bottom-left Location Notices.
- [ ] The placement contract reserves visible ruler chrome, including the horizontal ruler, vertical ruler, ruler corner, and scale bar.
- [ ] Tests or focused assertions cover the safe-slot geometry or resulting placement for representative ruler/scale-bar states.
- [ ] Existing canvas overlays continue to render without regressions.
- [ ] Design-system documentation remains aligned with the implemented placement rule.
close_reason: Implemented shared Canvas Notice Layout slots with ruler and scale-bar reservations, reused the ruler metric in chrome code, and verified with focused layout/ruler/chrome tests.
